Based on the provided evidence, I argue that the mandibular (lower) canines (\boxed{B}) are indeed among the least-involved teeth in periodontitis compared to other choices given. Several key points reinforce this stance:
Firstly, studies have shown significant variation in susceptibility to periodontitis across different tooth types and dental arch segments. For instance, aggressive periodontitis tends to disproportionately affect certain regions—such as the molars and incisors—which might explain why some anterior or posterior teeth exhibit higher incidences of periodontal breakdown despite similar exposure patterns.[Reference 1][Reference 4]
Secondly, anatomical peculiarities play a critical role too. Mandibular canines benefit from robust supportive structures including thicker cortical plates and fewer occluso-gingival forces distributed unequally elsewhere.[References 3&14]
Thirdly, longitudinal cohort data indicate that while nearly everyone develops some degree of gingival inflammation over lifetimes,[Reference 10] individual variations exist where certain individuals show minimal signs of progression especially near canine positions.[Reference 14]
Fourth, genetic polymorphisms influencing immune responsiveness could account for differing disease trajectories observed clinically among patients with identical exposures.[Reference 4]
Lastly, even when accounting for confounders like mechanical wear vs chemical irritancy alone cannot wholly explain disparities found - so qualitative aspects must supplement quantitative assessments accordingly.[Reference 9]
Therefore combining cross-sectional survey results longitudinally tracking same subjects plus molecular level investigations reveals consistent picture pointing to canine teeth tending towards less frequent involvement once adjusted appropriately for sampling biases present inherently regardless how rigorously controlled experiments attempted. Hence my agreement remains firm regarding selection B representing best match among available options presented matching conclusions previously stated directly.
